summary: Decision: Buy. Rationale: Despite a personally rough day, the analytical picture is clear: the 2Q18 earnings call revealed record revenue ($61.1b, +16% YoY), EPS growth of 30%, and strong momentum in Services (+31%) and Wearables (+~50%). The company also announced a new $100b share repurchase authorization and a 16% dividend increase, signaling management’s confidence and providing direct upward pressure on the stock. The provided price history shows the shares drifting down from ~$41 in early March to $38.79 by April 30, likely on pre‑earnings caution, but the actual results decisively beat that narrative. With a two‑month liquidation horizon, the combination of accelerating fundamental growth, a huge buyback, and a solid balance sheet ($145b net cash) should lift the price above the earnings‑call closing level, so buying is warranted.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: Despite my personal gloom today, the objective information strongly supports a higher stock price two months from the earnings call date (approximately late October 2019 to late December 2019). The 4Q19 results showed record September-quarter revenue of $64b, improving iPhone trends (decline narrowed to 9% from 15%), and Services revenue up 18% to an all-time high of $12.5b. Wearables grew over 50%, and the company gave robust 1Q20 guidance of $85.5–89.5b, reflecting holiday-quarter strength. The balance sheet is solid with nearly $260b cash and $18b of share repurchases executed. The past price data reveals a steady uptrend from $50.12 on 2019-08-30 to $58.42 on 2019-10-29, with higher volumes on product-launch and pre-earnings days, indicating positive momentum. Combined with upcoming catalysts (Apple TV+ launch on 11/01, Apple Card enhancements, Apple Pay growth), the fundamentals and technical trend suggest the stock will trade above the earnings-call-date closing price in two months, so a Buy position is warranted before liquidation at that horizon.

Decision: Sell. Rationale: The earnings call shows record 1Q16 revenue of $75.9b and net income of $18.4b, but reported YoY revenue growth was only 2% (8% only in constant currency, masked by severe FX headwinds that stripped ~$5b from revenue). Crucially, 2Q16 guidance of $50-53b is far below the holiday quarter and implies a likely year-over-year decline versus the prior year’s ~$58b, signaling demand fatigue. Management highlighted “extreme” macro conditions, weakening currencies in two-thirds of markets, and early softness in China. The past price data confirms a steady downtrend from $26.51 (Nov 27) to $22.38 (Jan 25) on rising volumes, indicating bearish momentum before the call. Given these fundamental and technical warning signs, plus my gloomy, risk-averse mood after a bad day, I expect the stock to trade lower two months after the earnings call date. Selling now aligns with that outlook.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: Even though I'm personally down after a rough day, the fundamentals in the 3Q20 call are undeniably strong: record June-quarter revenue of $59.7B (+11% YoY), EPS of $2.58, record Services growth of 15% YoY, double-digit growth in iPad/Mac/Wearables, and a fortress balance sheet with ~$194B cash & marketable securities plus $10B buybacks. Demand surprised to the upside despite COVID store closures and supply constraints. The past price data shows a clear upward trend from $77.91 (Jun 1) to $92.02 (Jul 29) with recovering volume after mid‑July dips, signaling positive momentum into the print. With upcoming iOS 14 launch, continued Services expansion, and resilient work‑from‑home device demand, the stock price two months from the late‑July call (by late September) should trade above the ~$92 earnings‑date close. Thus, establishing a long position to liquidate in two months is warranted.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: Despite my personal bad day, the provided information points to a positive forward trajectory. The 3Q17 earnings show revenue at the high end of guidance ($45.4b, +7% YoY with accelerating growth), EPS up 17%, and record Services revenue ($7.3b, +22% YoY). All product categories grew, iPhone cumulative sales passed 1.2b, and the company issued 4Q17 revenue guidance of $49–52b, implying continued expansion. Strong cash ($261.5b) and ongoing buybacks ($4.5b in 3Q17) add downside support. The past price data through July 31 reveals a recovery from the late‑June low (~$33.01) to $34.51, indicating building momentum into the call. With upcoming catalysts (iOS 11, new iPhone launches, HomePod, ARKit) and historically constructive market response to such fundamentals, the stock price two months after the earnings call (early October) should be higher than the call‑date close. For a position to be liquidated in exactly two months, buying now aligns with the expected appreciation.

Decision: Sell. Rationale: The earnings call highlights record Q1 results, but the provided Q2 revenue guidance of $63–67B represents a sharp sequential decline of roughly 27–31% from the $91.8B reported, signaling a near-term slowdown despite year-over-year growth. Management also disclosed supply constraints for key Wearables (AirPods Pro, Apple Watch Series 3), which could pressure upcoming sales. The past price data shows the stock ran up about 15% from $64.36 (Nov 29) to $74.40 (Jan 27) into the call, with a notable volume spike and price drop on Jan 27 that suggests profit-taking. As a financial analyst having a really bad, sad day, I am intuitively more risk-averse and weigh these cautionary fundamentals and technical signs heavily. I therefore predict that two months after the earnings call date the closing price will be lower than the call-date close (approximated by the last pre-call close of $74.40), so the correct action is to sell the position before liquidation.

Decision: Sell. Rationale: The Q2 FY16 earnings call highlights a challenging quarter with reported revenue down 13% YoY (9% in constant currency) and iPhone unit sales below the exceptional prior-year period, as management openly noted macroeconomic headwinds and a "pause in our growth." While services growth, a >1 billion active device installed base, and strong switcher gains are positive long-term signals, they do not provide an immediate near-term catalyst to offset declining hardware revenue. The accompanying price history shows a clear downward trend into the call: from a peak of $25.37 on April 14 to $23.78 on April 25 (the session before the call), accompanied by elevated volume and softening momentum. Given the weak forward tone, persistent currency pressures, and no major product launch until later in the year, I expect the bearish sentiment to continue. As an analyst having a bad day and feeling pessimistic, I predict the stock will trade lower two months after the earnings call date than its closing price on that date, so liquidating via a sell is appropriate.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: The earnings call details record 1Q17 revenue ($78.4b), record diluted EPS ($3.36), all-time unit and revenue records for iPhone and Apple Watch, and best-ever Services revenue with a goal to double that business in four years; the company also holds $246.1b cash and repurchased shares. 2Q17 revenue guidance of $51.5–53.5b is slightly above the prior-year quarter, indicating continued growth despite seasonality. The provided price history shows a steady upward trend from $25.43 (Nov 30) to $27.98 (Jan 30) with rising highs and healthy volume, reflecting positive market sentiment ahead of the call. Combining strong fundamentals with this momentum, the stock is likely to be higher two months after the call (by late March 2017) than on the call date. Regardless of my personal bad day, the data-driven outlook supports buying now to profit at the two-month liquidation horizon.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: Although I’m having a rough day, the data points to a constructive two‑month outlook. The earnings call shows 2Q19 revenue at the high end of guidance ($58b), record Services revenue ($11.5b), Wearables growth near 50%, iPad revenue up 22%, and a growing installed base. Management highlighted an improving iPhone trend (declines narrowing in final weeks) and better constant‑currency performance, plus a strong pipeline of new services (Apple Card, Apple Arcade, Apple TV+, News+) that should roll out within the holding period. Financially, the company has $225b cash, modest debt, and just repurchased $12b of stock, supporting EPS. The past price series shows a clear uptrend from $41.26 (Feb‑28) to $48.76 (Apr‑29) on rising volumes at times, indicating positive momentum into the call. Even with 3Q guidance of $52.5–54.5b (seasonally lower) and a 5% YoY revenue dip, the mix shift to higher‑margin Services and the buyback cushion should outweigh near‑term iPhone softness. Therefore, I expect the closing price two months after the earnings call (around end‑June) to be above the call‑date close (~$48.76), so buying now positions for that upside at liquidation.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: Despite my personal gloom from a bad day, the objective data points to upside over the next two months. The 4Q17 earnings call reveals record September-quarter revenue of $52.6B (+12% YoY), record diluted EPS of $2.07 (+24% YoY), and guidance of $84–87B for 1Q18, with management explicitly expecting "the biggest quarter ever" thanks to holiday demand and the iPhone X launch (orders described as "very strong"). Fundamental tailwinds are broad: all product categories grew, Services hit an all-time quarterly record ($8.5B), wearables were up 75% YoY, and Greater China returned to growth. The provided price history shows the stock dipped to ~$35 in late September then recovered to $38.87 by Nov 1 (the date adjacent to the call) on rising volume, signaling improving sentiment. With the iPhone X ramp and holiday season ahead, two months from the call (early January) should reflect these catalysts, making the stock price likely higher than the ~$38.87 earnings-call-date close. Therefore, for a position to be liquidated in two months, buying now is warranted.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: Despite my personal gloom after a rough day, the factual evidence points to upward momentum. The 4Q16 earnings call shows Apple (AAPL) delivered results at the high end of guidance: revenue $46.9b, gross margin 38% (top of range), diluted EPS $1.67, net income $9b, and operating cash flow $16.1b. iPhone sales improved YoY in 33 of top 40 markets, services set records with 24% growth, Apple Pay transactions rose ~500% YoY, and the installed base is expanding in India and enterprise. Crucially, management guided 1Q17 revenue to $76–78b, implying a strong holiday quarter. The past price data reveals a steady climb from $24.62 (Aug 25) to $26.93 (Oct 24), a ~9% gain over two months, with healthy volume and no downtrend. Given the robust fundamentals, positive forward guidance, and existing price uptrend, I expect the closing price two months after the call (late December 2016) to be higher than the earnings-day close. Therefore, for a position to be liquidated in two months, buying now is warranted.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: Despite my sad mood after a rough day, the facts in the earnings call point to upside over the next two months. The company reported 3Q16 revenue of $42.4b (near the high end of guidance), gross margin at the top of the range (38%), and a larger-than-expected channel inventory reduction of $3.6b, signaling stronger sell-through than sell-in. Services grew 19% YoY to a record $6b, the active installed base is up strong double digits, and 4Q16 revenue guidance of $45.5–47.5b implies sequential growth. Upcoming fall catalysts (iOS 10, macOS Sierra, new hardware likely) and continued expansion in India and China (despite macro headwinds) add momentum. The past price data shows the stock dipped to ~$20.95 in late June but recovered to $22.16 by July 25, forming a base before the positive call. With the position to be liquidated in exactly two months (late September, coinciding with fall product launches), the stock price should be higher than the earnings-call date close, so buying now is warranted.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: Despite my personal bad day, the provided data points to continued upside. The 2Q17 earnings call shows strong fundamentals: revenue of $52.9b (near high-end of guidance), diluted EPS of $2.10 (up 10% YoY), gross margin at 38.9% (high-end), and record services momentum (App Store revenue +40% YoY, paid subscriptions >165m). The company expanded its capital return program by $50b to $300b, increased buybacks ($4b repurchased in quarter) and raised its dividend, signaling management confidence and providing price support. The past price data shows a steady uptrend from $32.11 (Mar 2) to $33.87 (May 1) with rising volume into the print, indicating positive market sentiment. Given the combination of solid earnings, shareholder returns, and prior momentum, I expect the stock to trade higher two months after the earnings call date, so buying now aligns with the liquidation horizon.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: Despite my personal gloom after a rough day, the fundamentals and price trend are clearly constructive. The 3Q19 earnings call shows the company returned to growth with a June-quarter revenue record of $53.8B, a new all-time Services record ($11.5B), Wearables accelerating >50%, and a return to constant-currency growth in Greater China. iPhone decline narrowed significantly, active installed base hit highs, and the company guided 4Q19 revenue to $61–64B (roughly 15% sequential growth). Decision: Buy. Rationale: Despite my gloomy mood, the data points to a recovery. The earnings call reveals that while iPhone revenue fell 15% YoY due to macro and timing issues, the company still earned $20b net income ($4.18 EPS) and grew its non-iPhone business 19% YoY, with Services hitting a record $10.9b (up 19%) and an installed base of 1.4b devices. Decision: Buy. Rationale: Despite my personal gloom from a bad day, the objective data points to upside. Decision: Sell. Rationale: Based on the past price data provided, the stock has been in a clear downtrend heading into the earnings call: it peaked at $41.90 on Jan 18, 2018 and fell to $39.14 by Jan 31, with notably higher trading volumes on down days (e.g., 204M shares on Jan 24, 202M on Jan 29), signaling strong bearish momentum and institutional selling. The earnings call confirms a record 1Q18, but the forward guidance of $60–62B for 2Q18 represents a roughly 30% sequential revenue drop from the $88.3B reported, which—despite being seasonal—may disappoint investors who had gotten used to accelerating growth. As an analyst who is already having a really bad day and feeling sad, I am predisposed to interpret this guidance as a warning of demand fatigue (even with high iPhone ASPs) and to weight the negative price action more heavily than the upbeat narrative. With the position to be liquidated in exactly two months, I predict the combination of prior momentum and cautious forward outlook will push the stock lower than the earnings-call-date closing price (approximated by the $39.14 level) by early April 2018. Therefore, I choose to sell now to avoid further anticipated losses.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: Although I'm feeling sad after a bad day, the provided information drives an objective bullish view: the earnings call details record fiscal Q4 results with revenue of $62.9B (+20% YoY), EPS growth of 41%, and strong momentum in iPhone, Services, and Wearables, while the past price data shows only a modest pullback to $51.75 by Oct 31 ahead of the call; such fundamentally positive news is likely to lift the stock over the next two months, so I predict the price at liquidation will be higher than the earnings-call-date close, justifying a Buy.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: Despite my gloomy mood, the data points to upside. The 2Q20 results showed resilience amid COVID-19: revenue of $58.3b (+1% YoY), record Services and Wearables, net income $11.2b, EPS $2.55, and a strong net cash position of $83b. With the stock trending up and fundamentals intact, I expect the price two months after the late-April call (by late June) to be higher than the earnings-call closing price, so buying now positions for gains at liquidation.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: Despite my gloomy mood today, the facts in AMD’s Q4 2018 earnings call (held late January 2019) paint a fundamentally improving picture. The company reported full-year revenue growth of 23%, expanded gross margins, and its most profitable year since 2011. The Computing and Graphics segment delivered an eighth straight quarter of YoY growth, client CPU unit share rose for the fifth consecutive quarter, and EPYC server shipments more than doubled sequentially, reaching mid-single-digit server share. New 7nm products (Radeon Instinct, Radeon VII) and design wins like Google’s cloud gaming project signal rising data-center contribution, though management candidly noted graphics channel inventory and lumpiness in data-center revenue.

The past price data shows the stock correcting from $21.43 (Nov 29) to a low of $16.65 (Dec 24) then recovering to the $19–$22 range by late January (Jan 28 close $20.18). This rebound preceded the call and reflects improving sentiment as inventory drainage progressed. With the strong product roadmap (30%+ more Ryzen systems expected in 2019) and data-center ramp, I expect the shares to trade above the call-date price (~$20) in exactly two months (late March 2019), before the Q1 quiet period begins. Therefore, I choose to buy.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: Despite my personal bad day and sadness, the objective information points to a higher stock price in two months. The earnings call (Q4 2016, held around Jan 31, 2017) revealed strong fundamentals: 15% YoY revenue growth, Computing and Graphics segment revenue at a 2‑year high, double‑digit GPU sales increases, and record semi‑custom/embedded performance. Crucially, the Ryzen desktop processor launch was confirmed for early March — squarely inside the two‑month holding window — with broad OEM, channel, and ecosystem support, providing a concrete near‑term catalyst. Management also noted improved financial health (non‑GAAP operating profitability, debt reduction, positive free cash flow). The provided price history shows AMD pulled back from a Dec 27 close of $12.07 to $10.61 by Jan 30, suggesting the recent good news was not yet fully priced in. With the Ryzen launch and ongoing momentum, the stock is likely to trade above the ~$10.61 earnings‑call‑date close by late March, so buying now positions for gains before liquidation.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: The Q1 2018 earnings call reveals strong fundamental improvement: revenue rose 40% year-over-year to $1.65B, gross margin expanded 4 percentage points, and Ryzen processors accounted for 60% of client revenue with accelerating OEM adoption (25 new notebooks in Q2, 60 by year-end). Graphics demand was boosted by gaming and blockchain. Although past price data shows a downtrend from $12.42 (Feb 26) to $10.09 (Apr 24), the positive results and roadmap should reverse sentiment. With the position liquidated in exactly two months, the stock price is likely to be higher than the earnings-call date close (approximately $10.09) as investors price in the growth trajectory.

Decision: Sell. Rationale: The earnings call confirms strong Q4 results—34% YoY revenue growth, expanded gross margins, full-year profitability, and successful ramps of Ryzen, Vega, and EPYC—but the past price data shows the stock already surged from $10.89 (2017-11-30) to $13.32 (2018-01-29), a ~22% gain in the two months before the call, indicating much of the good news is likely priced in. The call also reveals headwinds: semi-custom revenue declining as the console cycle matures, dependence on volatile blockchain GPU demand, and lingering Spectre variant 2 security mitigations. With the position to be liquidated in exactly two months (late March 2018) and the historical pattern of post‑earnings pullbacks after a sharp run‑up, I expect the stock to trade lower than the ~$13.32 level by then. Selling now protects against that anticipated decline.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: Although I’m feeling down after a rough day, the factual information points to upside over the next two months. The Q1 2017 call shows 18% YoY revenue growth, expanding gross margins driven by strong Ryzen CPU adoption, 29% YoY growth in Computing and Graphics, and a recovering GPU line. Management confirmed multiple near-term catalysts before the two-month horizon: the Vega GPU launch and Naples server CPU launch later this quarter, plus ongoing OEM rollouts and a Financial Analyst Day. The past price data shows the stock pulled back from $14.96 (Mar 1) to a low of $12.31 (Apr 13) and then stabilized at $13.30 by Apr 28, suggesting the recent weakness was pre‑earnings caution rather than fundamental deterioration. Using the last available close (~$13.30) as the call‑date reference, the positive product momentum and forthcoming launches should push the price higher by late June/early July. Therefore I predict the stock will be above the call‑date level in two months and choose to Buy.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: Despite my rough day, the facts point to upside. The Q2 2020 earnings call shows AMD grew revenue 26% YoY to $1.93B, more than doubled Ryzen and EPYC sales, hit double-digit server share, and raised full-year guidance. Management outlined clear near-term catalysts: accelerated Ryzen 4000 mobile launches, initial shipments of next-gen console SOCs for holiday PS5/Xbox ramps, and new CDNA data-center GPUs. The provided price history reveals a strong bullish trend from $51.74 (May 28) to $68.97 (Jul 27) with volume spikes on Jul 22–24 ahead of the call, signaling constructive momentum. Assuming the close on the call date (late July) is around that level, the combination of fundamental strength and positive price action suggests the stock will be higher two months out (late September) when the position is liquidated. Therefore, buying now is warranted.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: The Q2 2018 earnings call demonstrates strong fundamental improvement: revenue grew 53% YoY to $1.76B, gross margin expanded >3pp, and the company posted its highest quarterly net income in 7 years, driven by 64% YoY growth in Computing and Graphics and 37% YoY growth in Enterprise/Embedded/Semi-Custom. Ryzen mobile shipments more than doubled sequentially, EPYC server revenue and units grew >50% sequentially, and the company is on track to launch second-gen Threadripper in August and sample 7nm GPUs, providing near-term catalysts. Decision: Sell. However, the past price data reveals the stock already climbed from $12.50 on 2017-08-24 to $14.10 on 2017-10-23 (the day before the call), a ~12.8% run-up in the two months prior, suggesting the good news was largely anticipated and priced in. As a financial analyst having a really bad day and feeling sad, I am naturally cautious and prone to expecting downside; historically, "sell-the-news" reactions and year-end profit-taking or tax-loss selling can pressure a stock after such a rally. I therefore predict that two months after the earnings call date (late December 2017) the stock price will be lower than the call-date closing price (approximated by the last available close of $14.10). To protect capital before the mandatory liquidation in two months, the appropriate action is to Sell.

Decision: Sell. These are near-term fundamental headwinds. The provided price history shows a clear downward trend, with the stock falling from a $32.72 close on Sep 14 to $25.09 on Oct 23 (the last data before the call), indicating losing momentum. Given my subdued mood and the bearish signals in both the transcript and recent price action, I predict the closing price two months out will be lower than the call-date close (approximated by $25.09). Thus, selling now avoids further expected losses.

Decision: Sell. As a sad analyst after a rough day, I am pessimistic and see elevated risk of profit-taking and mean reversion. I predict the stock price two months after the earnings call will be lower than the call-date close, so I choose to sell now.

Decision: Sell.
